q: What is Amazon Bedrock, and how is it different from self-hosting a model or using SageMaker?
a: Bedrock is a fully managed, serverless API over hosted foundation models from Anthropic (Claude), Amazon (Nova, Titan), Meta (Llama), Mistral, Cohere and others, so you call a model with an API key and no GPUs to provision. It scales to zero between calls and bills per token, versus self-hosting where you rent GPU instances 24/7 and own the serving stack. SageMaker is the control plane for training, deep fine-tuning, and hosting your own model artifacts on dedicated infra. The common split is Bedrock for the GenAI app layer (inference, RAG, agents) and SageMaker when you need to own the model and training loop.
fj: Bedrock and SageMaker are competing products for the same job.
fs: Bedrock is managed FM inference plus RAG/agents; SageMaker is train/fine-tune/host-your-own. They compose, not compete.

followups.q: If Bedrock scales to zero, why does anyone pay for Provisioned Throughput?
followups.a: On-demand scales to zero but offers no throughput guarantee — under load you can hit `ThrottlingException` and unpredictable latency. Provisioned Throughput reserves model units at a fixed hourly rate for steady, high-volume workloads needing stable latency, and it's the only way to serve custom (fine-tuned) models. You trade scale-to-zero economics for guaranteed capacity.

followups.q: A model you want isn't callable. What's the most likely cause that has nothing to do with code?
followups.a: Model access isn't on by default. You must request access per-model in the Bedrock console (`Model access` page) for your account and Region, and some are gated by license acceptance or are only reachable via an inference profile ARN. An `AccessDeniedException` on a fresh account is almost always un-granted model access, not IAM on the API.

followups.q: When would self-hosting on GPUs actually beat Bedrock economically?
followups.a: At very high, steady, predictable throughput an open-weight model on owned/reserved GPUs can undercut per-token billing, and self-hosting gives full control over quantization, custom architectures, and air-gapped deployment. The break-even is sustained utilization — if your GPUs sit idle, Bedrock's scale-to-zero wins. You also take on the serving, scaling, and ops burden Bedrock abstracts away.

q: How do you actually pick a model in Bedrock's catalog? Walk me through the decision.
a: Anchor on the task: reasoning-heavy or agentic work leans to Claude Opus/Sonnet, cheap high-volume classification or extraction to Nova Micro/Lite or Llama, and embeddings to Titan or Nova Multimodal Embeddings. Then weigh cost per million tokens, context window, latency tier, and whether multimodal (image/video) input is needed. Prototype two or three candidates against your own eval set rather than trusting leaderboards, because the Converse API lets you swap model IDs without rewriting code. Finally check the model is available in your Region (or via an inference profile) and that its license fits your use.
fj: Always pick the biggest, most capable model.
fs: Match model to task, then cost/latency/context. A 5-word classification on Opus burns money; Nova Micro does it for cents.

followups.q: Your eval set says two models are tied on accuracy. What's the tie-breaker?
followups.a: Cost and latency per request on your real traffic shape, then operational fit: context window headroom, whether one needs an inference profile, and Region/residency availability. If still tied, pick the cheaper-to-serve model and the one with the longer support runway — older models get deprecated, so check the model lifecycle/EOL notices.

followups.q: Why is trusting a public leaderboard a trap for model selection?
followups.a: Leaderboards measure generic benchmarks that rarely match your prompt distribution, domain, or output format, and they ignore cost, latency, and Region availability. A model topping MMLU can lose on your specific extraction schema. You need a representative gold set of your own tasks — Bedrock Evaluations exists precisely so you score candidates on your data, not someone else's.

followups.q: A teammate wants Claude Opus for a high-volume tagging job. Push back concretely.
followups.a: Tagging is recognition, not reasoning — Nova Micro/Lite or a small Llama does it at a fraction of the per-token cost and far lower latency. At high volume the price gap compounds into thousands per month for no accuracy gain. Reserve Opus for genuinely reasoning- or agent-heavy paths; route the cheap, repetitive work to a small model behind the same Converse interface.

q: InvokeModel vs the Converse API in Bedrock — what's the difference and which should you reach for?
a: InvokeModel sends a raw request body whose JSON shape is model-specific, so switching providers means rewriting the payload and parsing logic. The Converse API gives one unified schema — messages, system, inferenceConfig — that works across all message-based models, plus built-in multi-turn handling, streaming (ConverseStream), and tool use. AWS recommends Converse as the default; reach for InvokeModel only for a model or parameter Converse doesn't yet expose. Both authorize on the bedrock:InvokeModel action, so the API choice is about portability, not permissions.
fj: Converse is a different, more expensive product than InvokeModel.
fs: Converse is a unified wrapper over the same inference; it's portable across models and authorizes on the same action. Default to it.

followups.q: A model-specific parameter you need isn't in Converse's inferenceConfig. How do you pass it without abandoning Converse?
followups.a: Use the `additionalModelRequestFields` map on the Converse call to pass provider-specific parameters (e.g. Claude `top_k` or reasoning/`thinking` config) that the unified schema doesn't expose. You keep Converse's portable message shape and only the extra fields are model-specific, instead of dropping to raw InvokeModel and rewriting the whole payload.

followups.q: Streaming with ConverseStream — what's structurally different from a non-streaming response you must handle?
followups.a: ConverseStream returns an event stream, not one body: you iterate `contentBlockDelta` events to assemble text, watch `messageStop` for the `stopReason`, and read the `metadata` event for the `usage` block at the end. Tool-use arguments also arrive incrementally and must be reassembled before you execute. Your client code becomes event-driven rather than parsing a single JSON response.

followups.q: If both APIs authorize on bedrock:InvokeModel, how do you restrict access to just one model?
followups.a: Scope the IAM policy's `Resource` to that model's ARN (or the inference-profile ARN) rather than `*`. The action is shared, so least-privilege is enforced by resource ARN — and for cross-Region profiles you must also grant the underlying model ARNs in each destination Region, or routed calls get denied.

q: What does a Bedrock Knowledge Base handle for managed RAG, and what do you still have to engineer?
a: A Knowledge Base manages the RAG plumbing: it ingests documents from S3 (or other connectors), chunks them, embeds the chunks with a model like Titan or Cohere, writes vectors to a backing store, and at query time retrieves top-k chunks and returns them with citations. You still own data quality and source access control, the chunking strategy and metadata, ingestion/refresh scheduling, retrieval config (top-k, filters), and — most importantly — evaluation of answer quality and hallucination. 'Point it at S3 and you're done' ignores that chunking and freshness drive retrieval quality more than anything.
fj: The Knowledge Base is fully turnkey — no engineering left.
fs: It owns chunk/embed/retrieve/cite; you own chunking strategy, metadata, refresh, source ACLs, and eval.

followups.q: A document changed in S3 but the KB still returns the old text. Why, and how do you fix it?
followups.a: A Knowledge Base isn't live — it only reflects changes after an ingestion job (`StartIngestionJob`) re-syncs the data source. Until you re-run sync, edited or deleted S3 objects keep their stale vectors. Fix it by scheduling periodic ingestion (e.g. EventBridge on S3 events) and noting that incremental sync re-embeds only changed objects, so freshness is your responsibility, not the KB's.

followups.q: How do you stop a user from retrieving chunks they shouldn't see, when one KB serves many tenants?
followups.a: Don't rely on one index for isolation. Tag chunks with tenant/ACL values in metadata and pass a metadata `filter` in the retrieve request so only that user's chunks are eligible, or use separate Knowledge Bases per security boundary. The KB enforces no document-level ACLs by itself — retrieval-time filtering or KB partitioning is the engineering you own.

followups.q: RetrieveAndGenerate vs Retrieve — when do you want the raw retrieve call?
followups.a: `RetrieveAndGenerate` does retrieval, prompt assembly, and generation with citations in one call. Use bare `Retrieve` when you want the chunks but own the generation step — to inject custom prompt logic, route to a different model, apply your own guardrail wiring, or merge KB results with other context before calling Converse yourself.

q: Which vector store do you choose for a Bedrock Knowledge Base, and what drives the call?
a: Bedrock supports OpenSearch Serverless, Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector, Neptune Analytics, S3 Vectors, plus third parties like Pinecone, MongoDB Atlas, and Redis. OpenSearch Serverless is the default and scales well but has a hard minimum capacity floor (a few OCUs always on), making it expensive for small workloads. Aurora Serverless v2 with pgvector is dramatically cheaper at small-to-medium scale and lets you co-locate vectors with relational data. S3 Vectors (GA late 2025) gives near-zero idle cost, which suits dev/test or spiky retrieval. Drive the choice on scale, idle-cost tolerance, and whether you already run one of these stores.
fj: Just use OpenSearch Serverless; it's the only real option.
fs: OpenSearch Serverless has a costly always-on floor. For small scale I use Aurora pgvector or S3 Vectors to avoid paying for idle capacity.

followups.q: S3 Vectors is the cheapest option — what's the catch that stops you defaulting to it everywhere?
followups.a: S3 Vectors optimizes for low cost and massive scale (billions of vectors per index post-GA) but trades away the low p99 query latency of an always-warm engine like OpenSearch. It's ideal for spiky, cost-sensitive, or huge corpora and dev/test, but for latency-critical interactive RAG you may still want OpenSearch Serverless or Aurora pgvector. Match it to latency tolerance, not just price.

followups.q: You picked 1024-dim Titan embeddings, then want to switch the embedding model. What breaks in the vector store?
followups.a: Vectors are only comparable within the same model and dimension, so changing the embedding model invalidates the entire index — you must re-embed and re-ingest every chunk, not just new ones. The store's index dimension is fixed at creation, so a dimension change means a new index. Treat embedding-model choice as a migration-cost decision, not a flip.

followups.q: Why might co-locating vectors in Aurora pgvector beat a dedicated vector DB beyond cost?
followups.a: With pgvector your embeddings live alongside relational data, so you can do hybrid queries — vector similarity plus SQL `WHERE` filters, joins, and transactional consistency — in one engine you already operate and back up. That removes a separate system to secure and sync, and metadata filtering is ordinary SQL. The tradeoff is pgvector's ANN scaling ceiling at very large scale.

q: Bedrock Knowledge Bases offer fixed, semantic, and hierarchical chunking. When do you use each?
a: Fixed-size chunking splits on a token count with an overlap percentage — simple, predictable, and fine for uniform prose. Semantic chunking uses embedding distance between sentences to break at meaning boundaries, so related ideas stay together; it costs more to ingest but helps retrieval on heterogeneous documents. Hierarchical chunking stores small child chunks for precise matching but returns the larger parent chunk to the model, giving tight retrieval with broad context — strong for long technical docs and manuals. Match the strategy to document structure, then validate with a retrieval eval rather than assuming.
fj: Bigger chunks are always better because they give more context.
fs: Huge chunks dilute the embedding and hurt matching. Hierarchical (small child match, parent context) usually beats one big fixed chunk for technical docs.

followups.q: Mechanically, why does matching a small child chunk but returning the parent improve answers?
followups.a: A small child embedding is dense in a single idea, so search matches it precisely without the topic dilution a big chunk suffers. But the small chunk alone often lacks context, so hierarchical retrieval hands the model the larger parent — sharp recall from the child, enough context from the parent for grounded generation. It decouples retrieval granularity from generation context.

followups.q: Semantic chunking costs more at ingestion. What exactly are you paying for, and when is it worth it?
followups.a: Semantic chunking embeds candidate boundaries to find meaning shifts, so ingestion makes extra embedding calls (cost and time) versus a cheap token-count split. It's worth it on heterogeneous documents where fixed splits slice mid-thought — FAQs, mixed reports, interleaved topics. For uniform prose with stable structure, fixed chunking gives nearly the same retrieval at lower ingest cost.

followups.q: Why does chunk overlap matter, and what's the failure mode if you set it to zero?
followups.a: Overlap repeats a slice of adjacent text at chunk boundaries so a fact or sentence straddling a split still appears whole in at least one chunk. With zero overlap, a key sentence cut across a boundary gets fragmented, weakening both chunks' embeddings and risking that no single chunk carries the answer. Too much overlap inflates index size and cost, so it's a tuned trade.

q: How do Agents for Bedrock work? Explain action groups, orchestration, and tool use.
a: An agent pairs a foundation model with instructions, optional Knowledge Bases, and action groups — the tools it can call. Each action group declares functions via an OpenAPI schema or a function schema, and is backed by a Lambda (or returns control to your app to execute). The default orchestration is a ReAct loop: the model reasons about the user request, decides whether to call an action or query a KB, the result is fed back, and it iterates until it can answer. You can override this with custom orchestration (your own Lambda) when the default loop doesn't fit your control flow.
fj: An agent is just one big prompt that does everything in a single call.
fs: It's a ReAct loop: the model plans, calls action-group Lambdas / KBs as tools, feeds results back, and iterates. Action groups are the tool definitions.

followups.q: What does 'return of control' buy you versus backing an action group with a Lambda?
followups.a: With return-of-control the agent emits the chosen action and arguments back to your application instead of invoking a Lambda, so your own service executes the tool and sends results back. You use it when the tool needs code that can't live in Lambda — long-running work, on-prem systems, existing services, or human approval — while still letting the agent drive the loop.

followups.q: When would you reach for custom orchestration instead of the default ReAct loop?
followups.a: Custom orchestration replaces the built-in loop with your own Lambda that decides each step, so you reach for it when the default ReAct flow can't express your control: enforced step ordering, deterministic guardrail gates between tools, custom retry/fallback, or parallel calls. For standard plan-act-observe assistants the default loop is fine; custom is for bespoke state machines.

followups.q: OpenAPI schema vs function schema for an action group — does the choice affect tool selection?
followups.a: Functionally both declare tools; the function schema is a lighter way to define name, description, and parameters, while OpenAPI suits reusing an existing API contract. Either way, selection quality hinges on the descriptions, not the format — the model picks tools from those strings, so a precise OpenAPI `description` and a precise function description select equally well.

q: Your Bedrock agent keeps calling the wrong tool or hallucinating parameters. How do you debug it?
a: Most tool-selection failures are schema and instruction problems, not model problems. Tighten the OpenAPI/function schema so each action's description, parameter names, and required fields unambiguously state when to use it, and sharpen the agent instructions to set boundaries. Turn on trace in InvokeAgent to see the model's rationale, which action it picked, and the exact parameters it passed, so you can pinpoint where it went wrong. Validate inputs in the Lambda and return structured errors the agent can recover from rather than letting it guess.
fj: Swap to a bigger model and hope it picks the right tool.
fs: I read the InvokeAgent trace, then fix the action-group schema descriptions and agent instructions — most mis-calls are ambiguous tool specs, not model weakness.

followups.q: Two of your tools have overlapping descriptions and the agent flips between them. Concretely, how do you disambiguate?
followups.a: Rewrite each description to state the exclusive condition for use and call out the boundary explicitly — e.g. 'use to look up an EXISTING order by id; do NOT use to create orders.' Differentiate parameter names and mark required fields. If two tools genuinely overlap, merge them or add an enum parameter; the model can't pick correctly between two specs that both sound right.

followups.q: What's the value of returning a structured error from the Lambda instead of throwing?
followups.a: A structured error (e.g. `{'error':'order_not_found','hint':'ask user for a valid id'}`) is fed back into the loop so the agent can recover — re-ask the user or pick another path — rather than crashing the session. A raw exception or a 500 gives the model nothing to reason over, so it tends to hallucinate a retry. Make errors part of the contract.

followups.q: The trace shows the model reasons correctly but still passes a malformed parameter. Where do you look now?
followups.a: That points at the parameter spec, not selection: loosely typed or under-described parameters let the model guess format. Tighten the schema — exact types, `enum` for fixed sets, format hints in the description, and `required` flags — and validate in the Lambda, returning a structured error that names the bad field. Often a date or id format the schema never constrained is the culprit.

q: What do Bedrock Guardrails give you, and why apply them even with a well-aligned model?
a: Guardrails are a model-agnostic policy layer you attach to inference: content filters (hate, violence, sexual, misconduct, prompt-attack), denied topics defined in natural language, word filters, sensitive-information filters that block or mask PII, and contextual grounding checks that score whether a response is grounded in the provided source. They apply uniformly across any Bedrock model, agent, or Knowledge Base, so policy lives outside the prompt and survives a model swap. Even a well-aligned model needs them because your business rules (no investment advice, redact SSNs) are app-specific and must be enforced deterministically, with an audit trail.
fj: If the model is aligned, guardrails are redundant.
fs: Guardrails enforce app-specific policy (denied topics, PII masking) deterministically and uniformly across models — independent of whatever the base model happens to do.

followups.q: Guardrails screen input and output. Why do you need both, and what does each catch?
followups.a: Input screening catches prompt attacks, denied-topic requests, and PII before they reach the model context or your logs; output screening catches harmful or ungrounded content the model produced and masks PII before it reaches the user. A jailbreak slips past output-only checks; a model that surfaces a leaked SSN slips past input-only checks. Defense needs both ends of the call.

followups.q: Beyond content filters, what does an Automated Reasoning check add that grounding checks don't?
followups.a: Automated Reasoning checks validate a response against formal logical policies you encode, using mathematical proof to confirm it's consistent with your rules and explain why. Where contextual grounding scores 'is this supported by the source,' Automated Reasoning gives verifiable, deterministic policy compliance — useful for regulated logic like eligibility rules, not just hallucination scoring.

followups.q: Why is the audit trail from guardrails worth as much as the blocking itself?
followups.a: Guardrails emit per-request trace and metrics on what was filtered, masked, or blocked and why, which you ship to CloudWatch. That gives compliance an auditable record that policy was enforced deterministically — not 'we told the model to behave' — and lets you alarm on spikes in blocked events that signal abuse or a regression. Enforcement you can't prove isn't worth much in a regulated review.

q: What is a contextual grounding check in Bedrock Guardrails, and what does it actually catch?
a: It's a Guardrails policy that scores a model response against a reference source and the user query along two axes — grounding (is the answer supported by the source?) and relevance (does it address the question?) — and blocks or flags responses below thresholds you set. It targets RAG and summarization hallucinations: the model inventing facts not in the retrieved passages, or drifting off-topic. You tune the thresholds against your tolerance for false blocks, and AWS reports it filters a large majority of ungrounded RAG responses. It's a safety net on top of good retrieval, not a substitute for it.
fj: Grounding checks make hallucination impossible.
fs: It scores grounding and relevance against the source and blocks below a threshold — it sharply reduces RAG hallucinations but doesn't eliminate them, and bad retrieval still upstream-poisons it.

followups.q: How do you actually set the grounding threshold, and what does raising it cost you?
followups.a: Thresholds run 0 to 0.99; AWS examples often start near `0.7` and tune from there. Raising the grounding threshold blocks more ungrounded answers but also rejects more legitimate ones — false blocks where the source did support the answer — so you trade recall for safety. Tune against a labeled set measuring both hallucination caught and good answers wrongly blocked, not a guess.

followups.q: Why can't a grounding check rescue a RAG system with bad retrieval?
followups.a: Grounding scores the answer against the source you pass it — usually the retrieved chunks. If retrieval pulled wrong or empty chunks, an answer faithfully grounded in that bad context still passes while being wrong, or a correct answer gets blocked because the source lacks support. The check verifies faithfulness to source, not correctness of source.

followups.q: Distinguish a low grounding score from a low relevance score — they fail differently.
followups.a: A low grounding score means the answer asserts facts the source doesn't support — classic hallucination. A low relevance score means the answer may be true and grounded but doesn't address the user's actual question — drift or evasion. They're separate axes: you can be grounded but irrelevant (reciting unrelated source text) or relevant but ungrounded (confidently answering with invented facts).

q: You need RAG-quality answers but PII must never leak to logs or users. How do you wire Guardrails in?
a: Configure a guardrail with sensitive-information filters set to MASK (or BLOCK) for the relevant PII entities, plus any denied topics, then pass its guardrailIdentifier and version on every Converse/InvokeModel call so input and output are both screened. Mask on input to keep PII out of model context and logs, and mask on output to protect responses. Layer a contextual grounding check to keep answers tied to retrieved sources. Version the guardrail and pin a specific version in production so a policy edit can't silently change behavior, and review blocked-event metrics.
fj: Just tell the model in the prompt not to reveal PII.
fs: A prompt instruction isn't enforcement. I attach a versioned guardrail with PII filters set to MASK on input and output, applied at the API call, plus grounding checks.

followups.q: Why pin guardrailVersion to '3' instead of using the DRAFT version in production?
followups.a: DRAFT is mutable — an edit in the console silently changes production behavior with no review. Pinning a numbered version freezes the policy so changes go through a deliberate version bump you can review, test, and roll back. It's the same reasoning as pinning a dependency: production must run a known, immutable policy, not whatever someone last saved in the console.

followups.q: MASK vs BLOCK for a PII entity — when do you choose each?
followups.a: MASK redacts the entity (e.g. replaces an SSN with a placeholder) and lets the rest through, so the assistant stays useful while the secret never reaches logs or the user. BLOCK refuses the whole request/response when that entity appears — for when even acknowledging the data is unacceptable. Use MASK for usable redaction, BLOCK for hard 'cannot proceed' rules.

followups.q: The guardrail masks PII in the response, but it's still showing up in your logs. Where's the leak?
followups.a: Almost certainly you're logging raw model I/O before the guardrail runs, or logging the un-guarded request body yourself. The guardrail only masks what flows through its assessment — your own logging of prompts, retrieved chunks, or pre-guardrail payloads bypasses it. Audit every log statement to log post-guardrail text, and mask on input so context is clean too.

q: Which Bedrock embedding model do you choose, and what's the dimension tradeoff?
a: Titan Text Embeddings V2 is the text workhorse: up to 8,192 input tokens and a selectable output size of 256, 512, or 1,024 dimensions, so you can trade a little accuracy for cheaper storage and faster search. Nova Multimodal Embeddings handles text, images, video, and audio in one model with 256/384/1,024/3,072 options via Matryoshka representation learning, for crossmodal retrieval. Cohere embeddings are also available and strong multilingually. Smaller dimensions shrink the vector index and speed similarity search but lose some recall, so benchmark on your corpus before committing — and keep query and document embeddings on the same model and dimension.
fj: Always use the largest embedding dimension for best results.
fs: Bigger dims cost more storage and slower search for marginal recall. Titan V2 lets me pick 256/512/1024; I benchmark and often 512 is plenty.

followups.q: Matryoshka representation learning lets you pick dimensions. Why is that better than training a separate model per size?
followups.a: Matryoshka training packs information so the most important dimensions come first, meaning you can truncate a single embedding to 256/384/1024 and still get a usable vector — no separate model per size. You store one high-dim vector and shorten it where you need speed, or even re-rank with the full vector after a cheap short-vector search, all from one model and one ingestion.

followups.q: What concretely breaks if your query embeddings use a different model than your document embeddings?
followups.a: Embeddings from different models live in different vector spaces, so cosine/Euclidean similarity between them is meaningless — retrieval returns garbage even though no error is thrown. The same applies to mismatched dimensions. You must embed queries and documents with the identical model and dimension; this is why changing the embedding model forces a full re-index, never a partial swap.

followups.q: You picked 256 dims for cheap search but recall dropped. How do you recover quality without going back to 1024 everywhere?
followups.a: Use a two-stage retrieval: search cheaply with the 256-dim vectors to get a candidate set, then re-rank that small set with full-dimension vectors or a dedicated reranker (Cohere Rerank is available). You keep the index small and fast while restoring precision only where it matters. This is exactly the workflow Matryoshka embeddings are designed to enable.

q: Fine-tuning vs continued pre-training in Bedrock — when do you use which?
a: Fine-tuning trains on labeled prompt-completion pairs to teach a task, format, or tone (e.g. your support-ticket style), and needs only hundreds to thousands of good examples. Continued pre-training feeds large volumes of unlabeled domain text to shift the model's base knowledge toward a specialized domain like legal or biomedical, and needs far more data. Both produce a custom model that you can only serve via Provisioned Throughput, adding training, storage, and hosting cost. Before either, exhaust prompt engineering and RAG, since most 'the model doesn't know our stuff' problems are retrieval problems, not weight problems.
fj: Fine-tune the model so it knows our internal documents.
fs: Knowledge belongs in RAG, not weights — fine-tuning teaches task/format/tone on labeled pairs. For domain knowledge I reach for a Knowledge Base first.

followups.q: Why does baking knowledge into weights via fine-tuning age badly compared to RAG?
followups.a: Fine-tuned weights are a frozen snapshot — when a fact changes you must retrain and re-host a new custom model, and the model can still confidently emit the stale version. RAG updates by re-ingesting documents, so the new fact is live immediately with a citation. Weights are for stable skills (format, tone, task), retrieval is for volatile knowledge.

followups.q: When does continued pre-training genuinely beat heavy RAG plus prompting?
followups.a: When the gap is the model's base fluency in a specialized domain — dense legal/biomedical jargon, an internal DSL, or a low-resource language — not missing facts. RAG hands it documents, but if it can't parse the domain's vocabulary, continued pre-training shifts its base distribution so it reasons natively there. It needs large unlabeled corpora and the Provisioned Throughput cost.

followups.q: Your fine-tuned model performs worse than the base model on general prompts. What likely happened?
followups.a: Catastrophic forgetting / overfitting: a narrow or low-quality training set pushed the weights toward your task at the expense of general capability, or inconsistent labels got memorized. Fix it with a higher-quality, more diverse dataset, fewer epochs, and a held-out eval scoring both the target task and general ability — and re-confirm RAG/prompting couldn't have solved it without weights.

q: Provisioned Throughput vs on-demand in Bedrock — how do you decide, and what's the catch with custom models?
a: On-demand bills per token with no commitment and scales to zero, ideal for variable or unpredictable traffic and early development. Provisioned Throughput reserves capacity in model units at a fixed hourly rate (with 1- or 6-month commitments that discount the rate), giving guaranteed throughput and stable latency for large, steady workloads — and it's the cheaper unit economics once you're consistently busy. The catch: custom (fine-tuned or continued-pretrained) models can't run on-demand at all; you must buy Provisioned Throughput to serve them, so factor that hourly floor into any customization decision.
fj: Provisioned Throughput is just a discount you can toggle on anytime.
fs: It's a reserved hourly commitment for steady high volume — and the only way to serve a custom model, so fine-tuning carries a standing PT cost.

followups.q: How do you size the number of model units you need for Provisioned Throughput?
followups.a: A model unit guarantees a throughput quota in tokens per minute for that model, so size from measured peak demand: profile your on-demand traffic's tokens/minute at peak, divide by a unit's quota, add headroom. Undersize and you throttle; oversize and you pay for idle units. Load-test the real prompt/output token mix, since output-heavy traffic consumes units faster.

followups.q: Custom models force Provisioned Throughput. How does that change the ROI math on fine-tuning?
followups.a: Because a fine-tuned model can't run on-demand, you pay the PT hourly rate continuously the moment it's serving — a standing floor whether or not traffic flows. So fine-tuning only pays off at volume high enough to amortize that commitment; for low or spiky traffic, prompting plus RAG on an on-demand base model is far cheaper. Always price the PT floor into the decision.

followups.q: Traffic is steady but you don't want a 6-month lock-in. What's the middle path?
followups.a: Choose the no-commitment Provisioned Throughput (where available) at the higher hourly rate for flexibility, or buy a 1-month term rather than 6, accepting a smaller discount for less risk. You can also keep base-model traffic on-demand and reserve PT only for the custom model that requires it. Match commitment length to how confident you are the steady volume persists.

q: What are cross-Region inference profiles in Bedrock, and why would you use one?
a: An inference profile is an ARN you invoke instead of a bare model ID; a cross-Region profile lets Bedrock route a request across a set of Regions to absorb traffic bursts and raise effective throughput and availability without you managing failover. Geo profiles route within a geography (e.g. US or EU) for data-residency alignment, while global profiles let Bedrock pick the optimal commercial Region. Many newer models are only addressable through a profile, so 'pick a model' increasingly means model ID plus Region plus profile prefix. Use them for higher throughput ceilings and resilience to single-Region capacity limits.
fj: You always just pass the plain model ID to invoke a model.
fs: Many models are only reachable via an inference profile ARN. Cross-Region profiles spread load across Regions for throughput and resilience; geo profiles keep it in-region for residency.

followups.q: A global profile maximizes throughput by routing anywhere. Why might a regulated workload refuse it?
followups.a: A global profile lets Bedrock pick any commercial Region, so a request could be processed outside your data-residency boundary — unacceptable under GDPR or sovereignty rules. Those workloads use a geo profile (`eu.` or `us.`) confining routing to that geography, trading throughput flexibility for the guarantee inference stays in-region. Compliance, not performance, drives the choice.

followups.q: Your cross-Region profile call returns AccessDenied even though IAM allows the model. What's missing?
followups.a: Cross-Region routing means the request can execute in any destination Region in the profile's set, so your IAM policy must grant the underlying model ARN in every one of those Regions, plus the profile ARN itself — not just the home Region. Grant only the home model and a routed call to another Region is denied. List all destination model ARNs in the policy.

followups.q: Does invoking through a cross-Region profile change your token billing?
followups.a: No — pricing follows the model and the source Region's rate regardless of which destination Region actually serves the request; the routing is transparent for cost. What changes is effective throughput and resilience, since Bedrock can absorb bursts across Regions. You don't get charged extra for the routing, and you don't manage the failover yourself.

q: How does prompt caching work in Bedrock, and when does it pay off?
a: Prompt caching lets you mark stable prefixes of a request — a long system prompt, tool definitions, or retrieved context reused across turns — with cache checkpoints, so the model skips recomputing them on later calls within a short TTL (around five minutes). Cached input tokens are billed at a steep discount and latency drops sharply, since the expensive prefill is reused. It pays off for chat with a big fixed system prompt, agents with large tool specs, or document Q&A where the same document is queried repeatedly. It does nothing for one-off, fully unique prompts, and the cache expires quickly, so it's for hot, repeated context.
fj: Caching stores the model's answers so identical questions are free.
fs: It caches the input prefix (system prompt, tools, context) to skip prefill — not the output. Big reusable prefix wins; unique one-off prompts get nothing.

followups.q: You add a cache checkpoint but never get a cache hit. What's the most common cause?
followups.a: The prefix before the checkpoint isn't byte-identical across calls, or it's below the model's minimum — current Claude models like Sonnet 4.5/Opus require about 4,096 tokens per checkpoint (older 3.7 Sonnet needed 1,024). Any change above the checkpoint — a timestamp, reordered tools, a user id in the system prompt — invalidates the cache. Keep the cached prefix fixed and large enough to qualify.

followups.q: Why does cache ordering matter when you mix a 1-hour and a 5-minute TTL checkpoint?
followups.a: Bedrock requires longer-TTL cache entries to appear before shorter-TTL ones, so a 1-hour checkpoint must precede any 5-minute checkpoint in the prompt. The 1-hour TTL is only supported on newer Claude models (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus 4.5). Put your most stable context (system prompt, tools) in the 1-hour segment first, then volatile reused context in a later 5-minute checkpoint.

followups.q: The TTL is only about five minutes by default. How do you keep a cache warm across a long session?
followups.a: Each successful cache hit resets the TTL, so steady traffic within the window keeps the prefix warm indefinitely; the cache only expires after a gap longer than the TTL. For bursty sessions, either accept re-prefill on the cold call or, on supported models, use the 1-hour TTL for the stable segment. Don't engineer fake keep-alive calls — pay the re-prefill if traffic is genuinely sparse.

q: How do you evaluate model and RAG quality on Bedrock instead of eyeballing outputs?
a: Use Bedrock Evaluations. Automatic model evaluation runs curated or your-own datasets and scores predefined metrics like accuracy, robustness, and toxicity; an LLM-as-a-judge option scores quality dimensions at scale without humans; and human evaluation workflows route subjective judgments to reviewers. For RAG, Knowledge Base evaluation scores retrieval (are the right chunks pulled?) and generation (is the answer grounded and correct?) separately, which is key because a bad answer is usually a retrieval miss, not a generation miss. Build a representative gold set early and re-run it on every prompt, model, or chunking change to catch regressions.
fj: Read a few sample answers and ship if they look good.
fs: I run Bedrock Evaluations on a gold set — separating retrieval from generation metrics for RAG — and re-run on every change. Spot-checking hides regressions.

followups.q: Why is separating retrieval metrics from generation metrics the single most useful split in RAG eval?
followups.a: Because they have different fixes: a low retrieval score means re-chunk, change embeddings, or tune top-k/filters, while a low generation score on good chunks means prompt or model work. A blended 'answer quality' number tells you something's wrong but not where, so teams chase the wrong lever. Scoring retrieval and generation independently routes each failure to its actual cause.

followups.q: LLM-as-a-judge scales eval cheaply — what's the failure mode you must guard against?
followups.a: Judge bias and miscalibration: an LLM judge can favor verbose or confident answers, share blind spots with the model under test, and drift if you swap judges. Guard it by validating against a human-labeled subset, using a strong independent judge model, giving it a precise rubric, and re-checking agreement. Treat the judge as an instrument that itself needs calibration.

followups.q: When is human evaluation worth its cost despite automatic metrics existing?
followups.a: For subjective or high-stakes dimensions automatic metrics approximate poorly — tone, helpfulness, brand voice, nuanced correctness in regulated domains, or final launch sign-off. You use human eval to ground-truth and calibrate the cheaper automatic and LLM-judge pipelines on a sampled subset, then let automation carry the regression runs. Humans set the standard; automation scales it.

q: A security review asks: is our data sent to Anthropic, and how is it isolated on Bedrock?
a: No — Bedrock does not share your inputs or outputs with the model providers, and your data is never used to train the base foundation models; the model weights run inside AWS's Bedrock boundary. Data is encrypted in transit and at rest, and you can supply your own KMS customer-managed keys for custom-model artifacts and agent/session state. For network isolation, use VPC interface endpoints via AWS PrivateLink so traffic to Bedrock never traverses the public internet. Combine that with least-privilege IAM on the bedrock:InvokeModel action and CloudTrail logging for an auditable, isolated posture.
fj: Using Bedrock means our prompts train Claude and leak to Anthropic.
fs: Inputs/outputs aren't shared with providers or used to train base models. I add PrivateLink for private networking, KMS CMKs, and least-privilege IAM.

followups.q: PrivateLink keeps traffic off the internet, but a request still escaped your VPC. What did the endpoint policy miss?
followups.a: Exfiltration usually leaks via a different path — an alternate NAT route, a non-private SDK config, or credentials used outside the VPC. Lock it down with an endpoint policy plus IAM `aws:sourceVpce`/`aws:SourceVpc` conditions so Bedrock calls are denied unless they come through your endpoint. PrivateLink alone doesn't forbid the public path; the policy does.

followups.q: Where exactly do customer-managed KMS keys apply in Bedrock, given the base weights aren't yours?
followups.a: CMKs encrypt the artifacts you own: custom-model output from fine-tuning, agent and Knowledge Base session/state data, and ingestion inputs — not the base foundation-model weights, which AWS manages. So a CMK gives you control and revocation over your derived data and conversation state. Pair it with key policies so only your roles can decrypt, satisfying 'we hold the keys to our data.'

followups.q: What does CloudTrail actually capture for Bedrock, and what does it not?
followups.a: CloudTrail logs control-plane and API management events — who called which Bedrock action, from where, with what parameters — an auditable access record. It does not log prompt/completion content by default; for that you enable Bedrock model invocation logging to S3/CloudWatch. Use CloudTrail for the IAM/audit story and invocation logging (carefully, given PII) for content.

q: How do you monitor and control Bedrock token spend before it surprises you?
a: Cost on Bedrock is driven by input plus output tokens (output is typically several times pricier), so instrument first: every Converse/InvokeModel response returns a usage block with input, output, and cached token counts — log it per request, tagged by feature and user. Aggregate that with CloudWatch metrics and Cost Explorer, and set budgets and alarms. Then pull the obvious levers: route cheap tasks to Nova Micro/Lite, cap maxTokens, use prompt caching for reused prefixes, batch where latency allows, and Provisioned Throughput once volume is steady. The expensive surprise is usually an agent looping or an unbounded output, so alarm on tokens-per-request.
fj: Cost scales with the number of API calls, so just cap request count.
fs: Cost is tokens, not calls, and output tokens dominate. I log the per-response usage block, alarm on tokens/request, cap maxTokens, and route cheap tasks to small models.

followups.q: An agent's cost suddenly spiked 10x with the same user count. What's the prime suspect?
followups.a: A runaway ReAct loop — the agent re-calling tools or re-reasoning without converging — multiplies both input (growing history) and output tokens per session. Check the InvokeAgent trace for repeated tool calls and cap the loop with a max-iterations guard and tighter schemas. Unbounded `maxTokens` is the other usual suspect; both inflate tokens-per-request, which is why you alarm on it.

followups.q: Capping maxTokens limits output cost but can hurt quality. How do you cap without truncating real answers?
followups.a: Set `maxTokens` from the measured p95 of legitimate answer length plus headroom, not an arbitrary low number, and detect `stopReason='max_tokens'` to log truncations so you can tune. Different tasks get different caps — a classifier needs a handful, a summarizer more. Pairing per-task caps with truncation monitoring stops runaway cost without silently cutting valid responses.

followups.q: Batch inference is cheaper — why don't you use it everywhere?
followups.a: Batch trades latency for a lower per-token price, processing jobs asynchronously rather than in real time, so it's wrong for interactive paths where users wait. Use it for offline, latency-tolerant work — bulk embedding, overnight classification, dataset enrichment. Interactive chat stays on synchronous Converse; the cost lever there is small-model routing, caps, and prompt caching.

q: When do you use a Bedrock Agent versus orchestrating tool use yourself in application code?
a: A Bedrock Agent is worth it when you want AWS to manage the ReAct loop, session state, tracing, and the wiring of Knowledge Bases plus action-group Lambdas behind one InvokeAgent call — less glue code and a managed runtime. Rolling your own with the Converse API tool-use blocks gives full control over the loop, custom retry and fallback logic, multi-model routing, and tighter latency tuning, at the cost of writing and owning that orchestration. Choose the managed agent for standard tool-and-RAG assistants; choose hand-rolled orchestration (or a framework on Converse) when you need bespoke control flow the agent's loop can't express.
fj: Always use Bedrock Agents because hand-writing the loop is reinventing the wheel.
fs: Managed agents trade control for convenience. For bespoke retry/routing/loops I orchestrate Converse tool-use myself; for standard tool+RAG assistants the agent saves glue.

followups.q: Where does AgentCore fit relative to a managed Bedrock Agent and hand-rolled Converse orchestration?
followups.a: AgentCore (GA late 2025) is a framework-agnostic agent platform — runtime, memory, identity, gateway, observability — for operating agents at scale using any framework, model, or protocol. A managed Bedrock Agent is the lowest-glue path for standard tool+RAG; hand-rolled Converse gives max control; AgentCore sits between, managed runtime/ops with framework flexibility.

followups.q: You need parallel tool calls and per-tool retry budgets. Which approach, and why?
followups.a: Hand-rolled Converse tool-use, because you own the loop: you dispatch independent `toolUse` blocks concurrently, assign each tool its own retry/timeout/fallback budget, and decide stop conditions. The managed agent's default ReAct loop abstracts that away. The cost is writing and testing the orchestration yourself, but bespoke concurrency and per-tool resilience aren't expressible in it.

followups.q: What hidden operational work does a managed agent save that's easy to underestimate?
followups.a: Session state and memory across turns, structured tracing of every reasoning/tool step, KB-and-Lambda wiring, retries on tool failures, and request/response plumbing — all behind one `InvokeAgent`. Reimplementing that is significant, well-tested code: conversation persistence, trace plumbing, tool-result threading. For standard assistants that glue is what the managed agent earns its keep on.

q: Implement tool use with the Converse API directly. What does the loop look like?
a: You pass a toolConfig listing each tool's name, description, and JSON input schema. The model replies with stopReason 'tool_use' and a toolUse block naming the tool and arguments; you execute it in your code, then send the result back as a toolResult content block referencing the same toolUseId, and call Converse again so the model can incorporate it. You loop until stopReason is 'end_turn'. This is the same ReAct pattern a Bedrock Agent runs, but you own every step — error handling, parallel tool calls, and when to stop.
fj: The model executes the tool itself once you describe it.
fs: The model only emits a toolUse request; my code runs it and returns a toolResult with the matching toolUseId, then I re-call Converse. I own the loop.

followups.q: Why must the toolResult echo the exact toolUseId, and what breaks if you omit or mismatch it?
followups.a: The `toolUseId` correlates a result with the specific request the model made, which matters when the model issues several tool calls in one turn. Omit or mismatch it and Converse rejects the message or the model can't bind the result to its request, derailing the loop. Always thread the id from the `toolUse` block straight into the matching `toolResult`.

followups.q: Your loop never terminates — the model keeps emitting tool_use. How do you make it safe?
followups.a: Add a hard iteration cap and break with a fallback once exceeded, since you own the loop and Bedrock won't stop it for you. Also feed structured errors back so the model can recover instead of retrying blindly, and check `stopReason` each turn — only `end_turn` means done. Unbounded loops are both a correctness and a cost bug, so the max-iterations guard is mandatory.

followups.q: The model returns two toolUse blocks in one response. How do you handle them correctly?
followups.a: Execute both tools — concurrently if independent — and return both results as separate `toolResult` blocks in one user message, each carrying its own `toolUseId`, before calling Converse again. Don't drop one or send them in separate turns; the model expects every requested tool answered. This parallel-tool handling is the control hand-rolled orchestration gives you.

q: Architect a production support assistant on Bedrock: grounded answers, safe, observable. What are the pieces?
a: Ground answers with a Knowledge Base over your S3 docs (hierarchical chunking, Titan V2 embeddings, Aurora pgvector or OpenSearch Serverless by scale) and use RetrieveAndGenerate or an agent so retrieval and citation are managed. Wrap every model call in a versioned Guardrail — PII masking, denied topics, and a contextual grounding check — and pin the model via an inference profile for throughput and residency. For safety on actions, give the agent narrow action-group Lambdas with validated inputs and least-privilege IAM. Make it observable: log the usage block and InvokeAgent traces, alarm on tokens-per-request and blocked-guardrail events, and gate releases on a Bedrock Evaluations gold set. Use PrivateLink and KMS to keep it inside your network and key boundary.
fj: Just call the model with a good prompt and ship it.
fs: Production needs grounding (KB + citations), a versioned guardrail, an inference profile, traced/usage logging with alarms, eval gating, and PrivateLink/KMS isolation — the prompt is the smallest part.

followups.q: A support answer cites a doc but the fact is wrong. Walk the layers you'd check, in order.
followups.a: First retrieval: did the KB pull the right chunk, or is chunking/freshness/top-k surfacing a stale passage (re-run ingestion, check the chunk)? Then grounding: did the grounding check pass it, and is the threshold too lenient? Then generation: is the model misreading good context? A wrong cited fact is usually a retrieval or freshness miss, not invention — fix upstream first.

followups.q: How do you roll out a model or prompt change to this assistant without risking a silent regression?
followups.a: Gate the change on the Bedrock Evaluations gold set first, comparing retrieval and generation metrics against the baseline. Then ship behind a pinned guardrail version and a stable agent alias so production keeps the known-good config until the new one passes. The alias/version indirection lets you flip and roll back instantly; watch blocked-guardrail and token alarms for drift.

followups.q: What single alarm would most often catch this system degrading in production, and why?
followups.a: A spike in blocked-guardrail events — especially grounding failures — because it's the earliest signal that retrieval quality dropped (stale index, broken ingestion) or the model started drifting, before users notice wrong answers. Pair it with a tokens-per-request alarm for runaway loops. Grounding-failure rate maps most directly to the assistant's core promise: answers tied to your sources.

q: Bedrock vs SageMaker: give me the decision framework, not a one-liner.
a: Choose Bedrock when the job is consuming foundation models — inference, managed RAG via Knowledge Bases, agents, guardrails — and you want serverless scaling, fast time-to-value, and access to proprietary models you can't self-host. Choose SageMaker when you need control AWS Bedrock abstracts away: training from scratch or deep custom fine-tuning, novel architectures, your own model artifacts, classic ML pipelines, or specific hardware and serving configs. The boundary is ownership of the model and training loop: rent-and-call leans Bedrock, build-and-own leans SageMaker. A very common production shape is Bedrock for the GenAI app layer with SageMaker handling bespoke training or classic ML behind it.
fj: SageMaker is the old way and Bedrock replaces it.
fs: They serve different layers — Bedrock for consuming FMs (inference/RAG/agents), SageMaker for owning the model/training loop. Most stacks use both.

followups.q: You can fine-tune in both Bedrock and SageMaker. What pushes you to SageMaker's training instead?
followups.a: Bedrock fine-tuning is a managed job with limited knobs on a supported FM, served only via Provisioned Throughput. Go to SageMaker when you need control it abstracts away — full hyperparameter access, custom training loops, novel architectures, distributed training, owning the artifact, or specific accelerator configs. If managed fine-tuning on a hosted FM suffices, stay in Bedrock.

followups.q: Give a concrete production shape where Bedrock and SageMaker genuinely run together.
followups.a: A support app where Bedrock fronts the GenAI layer — KB-grounded RAG, an agent, guardrails — while a SageMaker-hosted classic ML model scores ticket priority or churn, and SageMaker runs a custom domain model the agent calls as a tool. Bedrock handles the conversational/FM layer; SageMaker owns the bespoke and classic-ML models. They compose along the model-ownership boundary.

followups.q: A team insists on SageMaker for everything to 'keep control.' What's the cost of that purity?
followups.a: Owning training and serving for tasks a hosted FM already does well means standing GPU cost, MLOps burden, and slow time-to-value — plus you can't self-host proprietary models like Claude. The control is real but often unused; most GenAI app work is consuming FMs, which Bedrock does serverless and cheaper to operate. Reserve SageMaker for where ownership buys something, not as a default.
